name: tenacity

metadata.name: tenacity
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License

documentation: Tenacity is a general-purpose retrying library to simplify the task of adding retry behavior to just about anything.

functions.name: tenacity.nap.sleep
documentation:
Sleep strategy that delays execution for a given number of seconds.

This is the default strategy, and may be mocked out for unit testing.

classes.name: tenacity.nap.sleep_using_event
documentation: Sleep strategy that waits on an event to be set.

documentation: Stop if any of the stop condition is valid.

classes.name: tenacity.wait.wait_base
documentation: Abstract base class for wait strategies.

classes.name: tenacity.wait.wait_fixed
documentation: Wait strategy that waits a fixed amount of time between each retry.

classes.name: tenacity.wait.wait_none
documentation: Wait strategy that doesn't wait at all before retrying.

classes.name: tenacity.wait.wait_random
documentation: Wait strategy that waits a random amount of time between min/max.

classes.name: tenacity.wait.wait_combine
documentation: Combine several waiting strategies.

classes.name: tenacity.wait.wait_chain
documentation:
Chain two or more waiting strategies.

If all strategies are exhausted, the very last strategy is used
thereafter.

For example:

```
@retry(wait=wait_chain(*[wait_fixed(1) for i in range(3)] +
                       [wait_fixed(2) for j in range(5)] +
                       [wait_fixed(5) for k in range(4)))
def wait_chained():
    print("Wait 1s for 3 attempts, 2s for 5 attempts and 5s
           thereafter.")
```

classes.name: tenacity.wait.wait_incrementing
documentation:
Wait an incremental amount of time after each attempt.

Starting at a starting value and incrementing by a value for each attempt
(and restricting the upper limit to some maximum value).

classes.name: tenacity.wait.wait_exponential
documentation:
Wait strategy that applies exponential backoff.

It allows for a customized multiplier and an ability to restrict the
upper and lower limits to some maximum and minimum value.

The intervals are fixed (i.e. there is no jitter), so this strategy is
suitable for balancing retries against latency when a required resource is
unavailable for an unknown duration, but *not* suitable for resolving
contention between multiple processes for a shared resource. Use
wait_random_exponential for the latter case.

classes.name: tenacity.wait.wait_random_exponential
documentation:
Random wait with exponentially widening window.

An exponential backoff strategy used to mediate contention between multiple
uncoordinated processes for a shared resource in distributed systems. This
is the sense in which "exponential backoff" is meant in e.g. Ethernet
networking, and corresponds to the "Full Jitter" algorithm described in
this blog post:

<https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/architecture/exponential-backoff-and-jitter/>

Each retry occurs at a random time in a geometrically expanding interval.
It allows for a custom multiplier and an ability to restrict the upper
limit of the random interval to some maximum value.

Example:

```
wait_random_exponential(multiplier=0.5,  # initial window 0.5s
                        max=60)          # max 60s timeout
```

When waiting for an unavailable resource to become available again, as
opposed to trying to resolve contention for a shared resource, the
wait_exponential strategy (which uses a fixed interval) may be preferable.

classes.name: tenacity.wait.wait_exponential_jitter
documentation:
Wait strategy that applies exponential backoff and jitter.

It allows for a customized initial wait, maximum wait and jitter.

This implements the strategy described here:
https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/retry-strategy

The wait time is min(initial * 2**n + random.uniform(0, jitter), maximum)
where n is the retry count.

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_base
documentation: Abstract base class for retry strategies.

classes.name: tenacity.retry._retry_never
documentation: Retry strategy that never rejects any result.

classes.name: tenacity.retry._retry_always
documentation: Retry strategy that always rejects any result.

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_if_exception
documentation: Retry strategy that retries if an exception verifies a predicate.

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_if_exception_type
documentation: Retries if an exception has been raised of one or more types.

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_if_not_exception_type
documentation: Retries except an exception has been raised of one or more types.

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_unless_exception_type
documentation: Retries until an exception is raised of one or more types.

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_if_exception_cause_type
documentation:
Retries if any of the causes of the raised exception is of one or more types.

The check on the type of the cause of the exception is done recursively (until finding
an exception in the chain that has no `__cause__`)

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_if_result
documentation: Retries if the result verifies a predicate.

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_if_not_result
documentation: Retries if the result refutes a predicate.

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_if_exception_message
documentation: Retries if an exception message equals or matches.

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_if_not_exception_message
documentation: Retries until an exception message equals or matches.

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_any
documentation: Retries if any of the retries condition is valid.

classes.name: tenacity.retry.retry_all
documentation: Retries if all the retries condition are valid.

functions.name: tenacity.retry
documentation:
Wrap a function with a new `Retrying` object.

:param dargs: positional arguments passed to Retrying object
:param dkw: keyword arguments passed to the Retrying object

classes.name: tenacity.TryAgain
documentation: Always retry the executed function when raised.

classes.name: tenacity.BaseAction
documentation:
Base class for representing actions to take by retry object.

Concrete implementations must define:
- __init__: to initialize all necessary fields
- REPR_FIELDS: class variable specifying attributes to include in repr(self)
- NAME: for identification in retry object methods and callbacks

classes.name: tenacity.RetryError
documentation: Encapsulates the last attempt instance right before giving up.

classes.name: tenacity.AttemptManager
documentation: Manage attempt context.

methods.name: tenacity.BaseRetrying.copy
documentation: Copy this object with some parameters changed if needed.

methods.name: tenacity.BaseRetrying.statistics
documentation:
Return a dictionary of runtime statistics.

This dictionary will be empty when the controller has never been
ran. When it is running or has ran previously it should have (but
may not) have useful and/or informational keys and values when
running is underway and/or completed.

:::{warning}
The keys in this dictionary **should** be some what
stable (not changing), but there existence **may**
change between major releases as new statistics are
gathered or removed so before accessing keys ensure that
they actually exist and handle when they do not.
:::

:::{note}
The values in this dictionary are local to the thread
running call (so if multiple threads share the same retrying
object - either directly or indirectly) they will each have
there own view of statistics they have collected (in the
future we may provide a way to aggregate the various
statistics from each thread).
:::

methods.name: tenacity.BaseRetrying.wraps
documentation:
Wrap a function for retrying.

:param f: A function to wraps for retrying.

classes.name: tenacity.Retrying
documentation: Retrying controller.

classes.name: tenacity.Future

methods.name: tenacity.Future.failed
documentation: Return whether a exception is being held in this future.

methods.name: tenacity.Future.construct
documentation: Construct a new Future object.
Encapsulates a (future or past) attempted call to a target function.

classes.name: tenacity.RetryCallState
documentation: State related to a single call wrapped with Retrying.
